_about: Labelled eval set for image<->text retrieval (Phase 3a of #162). image_notes: the answer lives in the IMAGE (note `text` deliberately does NOT name it), so a correct retrieval of `query` must come from the image vector, not the text. `search_term` is resolved to a real current Wikimedia Commons image via the MediaWiki API (robust to filename drift). text_notes: answerable by text, used to compare the CLIP text encoder against the MiniLM baseline.

image_notes.query: the Krebs cycle / citric acid cycle pathway diagram
a labelled diagram of the heart chambers and valves
a map of the world showing the continents
evaporation, condensation and precipitation water cycle
the periodic table of the chemical elements
a labelled plant cell with chloroplasts and a vacuole
the human skeleton bones, front view
a diagram of a neuron with axon and dendrites
the double helix structure of DNA
a cross-section of the human eye showing the retina and lens
a photograph of a domestic cat
a photo of the Eiffel Tower in Paris
a cross-section of a volcano showing the magma chamber
a simple resistor and capacitor circuit schematic
a photo of an orange and black monarch butterfly

text_notes.text: The mitochondrion is a membrane-bound organelle that generates most of a cell's supply of ATP through oxidative phosphorylation.
text_notes.query: which organelle produces most of the cell's energy

text_notes.text: Photosynthesis converts light energy, water, and carbon dioxide into glucose and oxygen in the chloroplasts of green plants.
text_notes.query: how plants turn sunlight into chemical energy

text_notes.text: Newton's second law states that the acceleration of an object is proportional to the net force acting on it and inversely proportional to its mass.
text_notes.query: the relationship between force, mass, and acceleration

text_notes.text: The French Revolution began in 1789 and led to the end of the absolute monarchy and the rise of the French Republic.
text_notes.query: when did the monarchy fall in France

text_notes.text: Insulin is a hormone produced by the pancreas that regulates blood glucose by promoting its uptake into cells.
text_notes.query: the hormone that controls blood sugar

text_notes.text: A leap year has 366 days, adding February 29th, and occurs roughly every four years to keep the calendar aligned with Earth's orbit.
text_notes.query: why we add an extra day in February some years

text_notes.text: The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately 299,792 kilometres per second, a universal physical constant denoted c.
text_notes.query: how fast light travels in empty space

text_notes.text: Plate tectonics describes the slow movement of large sections of Earth's lithosphere, causing earthquakes, volcanoes, and mountain formation.
text_notes.query: what causes earthquakes and mountain ranges

text_notes.text: Vaccines work by training the immune system to recognise and respond to a specific pathogen without causing the disease itself.
text_notes.query: how do vaccines protect against illness

text_notes.text: The boiling point of water at sea level is 100 degrees Celsius, decreasing at higher altitudes where atmospheric pressure is lower.
text_notes.query: the temperature at which water boils

text_notes.text: Supply and demand is the economic model where the price of a good settles where the quantity buyers want equals the quantity sellers offer.
text_notes.query: what determines prices in a free market

text_notes.text: DNA encodes genetic information in sequences of four nucleotide bases: adenine, thymine, guanine, and cytosine.
text_notes.query: the four building blocks of the genetic code
